jokes.text: Ilana gives her full financial backstory unprompted at what appears to be a job interview or office tour, including the bed bug shutdown and 'I'm clean now. I'm... I'm fully clean.'
jokes.setup: Professional context (office/interview setting) where brevity and polish are expected norms
jokes.punchline: Ilana unloads a rambling, TMI work history culminating in 'I'm clean now' — the pause-laden delivery implying she needed to convince herself as much as the interviewer
jokes.explanation: Strong character establishment joke — Ilana's obliviousness to professional norms is core to her persona. The triple hesitation on 'I'm clean now. I'm... I'm fully clean.' sells that she knows it sounds bad but can't stop herself. Good opener but not the sharpest version of this type of joke.

jokes.text: The interviewer's reaction: 'Uh, wow, uh, usually, we don't get everybody's back story. That's, uh, surprising. Living on the edge.'
jokes.setup: Ilana's oversharing has clearly made everyone uncomfortable
jokes.punchline: The interviewer's stunned, polite understatement — 'Living on the edge' as a way of processing the chaos without confronting it
jokes.explanation: Competent straight-man reaction that validates the first joke. 'Living on the edge' is a decent deflection, but the interviewer character isn't distinct enough for this to land harder. Functional but forgettable.

jokes.text: Ilana negotiates her rent DOWN at a co-working space — 'It'd be $500 a month.' 'Okay, I was thinking more like $1,000.'
jokes.setup: Standard negotiation scene where the client tries to get a lower price
jokes.punchline: Ilana counter-offers HIGHER, believing she's negotiating UP to something better — pure Ilana logic that a higher price means a better deal for her
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The inversion is perfectly constructed: every expectation of negotiation is flipped in one line. The rep's genuine excitement ('Whoa, that would get you a way bigger office') makes it funnier by playing it straight. Absolutely character-specific — Ilana's combination of confidence and economic illiteracy makes this joke only work coming from her. Economy is near-perfect: zero wasted words. Justifiably scores 8.5.

jokes.text: Ilana realizes she thought she was interviewing for the office manager job, not renting a desk. 'We're the office manager.'
jokes.setup: Ilana's confusion about why she's there has been building; now revealed she had the entire premise wrong
jokes.punchline: 'We're the office manager' — delivered flatly, as if this is obvious, while Ilana's world collapses
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the negotiation confusion. The deadpan 'We're the office manager' is sharp and well-timed. Rewatch bonus because knowing this going in makes joke #3 even funnier — she negotiated herself into paying MORE for a desk she didn't need.

jokes.text: Abbi shows up at the wrong address — '495 Broadway.' '490.' 'Pffft! Classic me.'
jokes.setup: Abbi arrives, off-screen mishap implied — she went to the wrong building
jokes.punchline: 'Classic me' — complete self-awareness with zero self-correction, delivered with a rueful laugh
jokes.explanation: Quick, efficient character beat. 'Classic me' is a well-worn phrase elevated slightly by Abbi's resigned self-acceptance. Functional, character-consistent, but not especially inventive.

jokes.text: Abbi calls out the YouWork business model to the rep's face: storing people in closets, luring them with 'high-end perks' that are 'a bag of beer in a trash can under a counter.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just experienced the gap between YouWork's branding and its reality
jokes.punchline: She articulates it with escalating specificity, landing on the precise, absurd detail of 'a bag of beer in a trash can under a counter' as the contrasting 'perk'
jokes.explanation: Sharp WeWork satire. The specificity of 'a bag of beer in a trash can under a counter' is earned and funny. Abbi is less the vehicle for this joke than Ilana typically is — it works as observational comedy more than character comedy, which slightly limits it.

jokes.text: Rep responds to Abbi's takedown of the YouWork scam with 'That is fucking genius.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just described the business model as predatory and cynical
jokes.punchline: The rep confirms it by treating her critique as a compliment — he genuinely means it
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — four words cap the scene. The joke works because it reframes Abbi's criticism as the rep's origin story. It's the ideal button on the YouWork cold open: the satire validates itself. Not mind-blowing but very cleanly executed.

jokes.text: Fernando the super catalogues what's clogging Abbi's toilet: 'toilet paper's not gonna make it through these old pipes, much less your tampons and maxi pads with wings for heavy flow'
jokes.setup: Standard landlord-tenant plumbing complaint conversation
jokes.punchline: Fernando escalates from 'toilet paper' to an unsolicited, specific inventory of Abbi's menstrual products, including the sub-brand detail 'wings for heavy flow'
jokes.explanation: The cringe escalation from toilet paper to 'maxi pads with wings for heavy flow' is well-paced. The specificity is the joke — Fernando has clearly been taking notes. Works both as a cringe moment and as setup for Abbi's correction.

jokes.text: Abbi's correction: 'Okay, first of all, I have an extra-heavy flow.'
jokes.setup: Fernando has accused her of heavy-flow products; the audience expects her to deny the tampons
jokes.punchline: Abbi's first objection is not the privacy violation but the factual error — she has an EXTRA-heavy flow and demands that be acknowledged
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion. The expected beat is embarrassment and denial; instead Abbi doubles down on the overshare to correct the record. It's a great character moment — Abbi is direct about her body to the point of absurdity — and the 'first of all' framing makes it feel like she's been waiting to set this straight. Justifies the 8.0: it's a model of misdirection-through-character-specificity.

jokes.text: Fernando reveals that all residents use a separate garbage can for used toilet paper and 'they don't make a stink about it.' Beat. 'Stink.'
jokes.setup: Fernando is explaining the building's toilet paper disposal protocol
jokes.punchline: He uses 'stink' idiomatically, then repeats it with apparent awareness of — or complete unawareness of — the double meaning, before continuing
jokes.explanation: A gentle pun, the kind of throwaway that lands as a small groan-chuckle. The ambiguity of whether Fernando intended it is the best part. Weak pun humor, but the beat timing saves it.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'So I can't poop, wipe, and flush in my own apartment?'
jokes.setup: Fernando has delivered several absurd restrictions on toilet use; Abbi tries to summarize
jokes.punchline: The blunt reduction of all modern plumbing expectations to its three core verbs — poop, wipe, flush — stated flatly as a trilogy of rights being denied
jokes.explanation: Very clean distillation of the absurdity. The tricolon structure — poop, wipe, flush — sounds like a legal brief, which is funny. Good economy, broadly quotable, and captures Abbi's exhausted disbelief well.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I mean, that's some third-world shit.' Fernando: 'You mean developing nation.' Abbi: 'I-I-I did mean that. That is... That is right.'
jokes.setup: Abbi uses an outdated/offensive term in the heat of frustration
jokes.punchline: Fernando — a person of color — corrects her; Abbi's stammering overcorrection ('I-I-I did mean that. That is... That is right.') performs the cringe of someone who knows they messed up but can't stop talking
jokes.explanation: Sharp social-comedy moment. The show uses it to show Abbi's self-consciousness without belaboring the point. Fernando's calm correction is dignified, and Abbi's triple stutter is perfectly calibrated cringe. The pivot to Fernando's pride in his 'shit bucket' upbringing immediately after is an excellent tonal flip.

jokes.text: Fernando: 'Well, I grew up using a shit bucket. And I did not mean that in a bad way. I meant it in a great way. Growing up like that is dope.'
jokes.setup: The conversation has made 'shit bucket' a loaded, shameful term
jokes.punchline: Fernando reclaims it with genuine pride — 'I meant it in a great way. Growing up like that is dope.' — a total reframe that's both funny and oddly touching
jokes.explanation: Fernando's pivot is the best Fernando joke in the episode. 'Growing up like that is dope' could be condescending from a different character or writer, but the specificity and his complete sincerity makes it land as character comedy rather than mockery. It also plants the shit bucket as a running element.

jokes.text: Abbi immediately decides to get a shit bucket: 'You know what? I'm gonna get one. I'm just gonna get one. I'll get a shit bucket.'
jokes.setup: Fernando has just praised his shit bucket childhood; the conversation was about a plumbing PROBLEM
jokes.punchline: Abbi, susceptible to any passionate endorsement, immediately plans to adopt the solution she was complaining about — before Fernando can explain it's only for toilet paper
jokes.explanation: Plants the payoff joke at 19:16 beautifully. On first watch it reads as Abbi being impulsive and too easily influenced; on rewatch you realize this is the exact moment she misunderstands the shit bucket's purpose, which makes the later revelation even funnier. Strong structural work.

jokes.text: Ilana can't find her interview location: 'All right, where the fuck was this interview supposed to be? Shit, phone...' followed by her dropping/fumbling her phone
jokes.setup: Ilana, already established as chaotic, is navigating the city for an interview
jokes.punchline: She loses track of the address and her phone in the same breath — the chaos compounding immediately
jokes.explanation: Functional chaos gag, works more as physical performance than writing. Inferred from transcript — the 'Shit, phone...' suggests a physical drop or fumble. Serviceable but not inventive.

jokes.text: Ilana looks around at the city chaos around her and says simply: 'I love this city.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just dropped her phone amid the noise and chaos of a NYC street
jokes.punchline: Beatific, totally sincere love declaration — the joke is that the chaos is WHY she loves it, not despite it
jokes.explanation: Deceptively simple. Four words, maximum economy. The gap between the chaos and the sincere love is the joke, and the timing (immediately after the phone fumble) makes it land. Very Ilana, very Broad City in spirit. Economy score is a 10 because it could not be shorter.

jokes.text: Series of collisions and 'sorry / oh shit / sorry / oh no' as Abbi navigates a busy sidewalk, clearly bumping into multiple people
jokes.setup: Abbi is walking through NYC with her items
jokes.punchline: The rapid-fire accumulation of collisions — four separate impacts compressed into seconds — becomes its own escalating bit
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy inferred from the staccato transcript. Works better visually than on paper. Abbi's NYC-scramble energy is consistent character work, but four 'sorry' beats is one too many.

jokes.text: Abbi, after all those collisions, passes a newcomer and says 'I just moved here.' The stranger responds flatly: 'Cool.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is visibly struggling to navigate the city
jokes.punchline: She over-explains herself to a stranger who could not care less — 'Cool.' is the perfect NYC non-response
jokes.explanation: 'Cool.' is a perfect NYC stranger response. One word, no affect, complete indifference — captures the city's emotional temperature. Economy is high; the joke is short and doesn't overstay its welcome.

jokes.text: Ilana spots and recognizes Linda Lodi on the street — the reunion energy and Ilana's genuine excitement at bumping into her former boss
jokes.setup: Random NYC street encounter
jokes.punchline: Ilana treats a minor ex-coworker like a long-lost best friend — 'Linda freakin' Lodi!' — while Linda's reaction is warm but proportionate
jokes.explanation: Mostly setup material that pays off through Ilana's opportunism later. The middle name energy of 'Linda freakin' Lodi' is good character voice. Functions more as exposition than joke.

jokes.text: Linda's screenplay is titled 'We Lost Grandma at the Water Park' — she insists it's 'a drama, actually.'
jokes.setup: Ilana says the title 'sounds hilarious'
jokes.punchline: Linda corrects: it's a drama — complete sincerity, zero awareness of the absurdity of the title
jokes.explanation: Strong joke. 'We Lost Grandma at the Water Park' is an objectively funny title; the comedy is in Linda's total seriousness about it. The structure is textbook — the title does the setup work, the 'it's a drama, actually' is the punchline. High quotability because the title itself is memorable. Near 8-range: the joke is original, well-constructed, and the character specificity (deluded aspiring filmmaker) is clear even in a brief scene.

jokes.text: Linda's complaint: she wants a place to smoke while working, and argues that anti-smoking policies are discriminatory: 'Anti-smoking is discriminatory.'
jokes.setup: Linda complains about being kicked out for smoking
jokes.punchline: Ilana's response — 'You know, I never thought about it like that.' — validates the absurd logic completely; Ilana treats it as genuine civil rights discourse
jokes.explanation: Works because Ilana's earnest agreement ('You know, I never thought about it like that') is funnier than any pushback would be. The joke seeds SheWork's mission statement and characterizes both women efficiently.

jokes.text: Ilana reveals her SheWork pricing: '50 cents a minute.' Linda immediately agrees and pays for 40-45 minutes on the spot.
jokes.setup: Ilana is pitching her new 'business' — an open-air sidewalk spot near charging stations
jokes.punchline: She charges $0.50/minute ($30/hour) for what is literally just a public sidewalk with free city charging stations — and Linda immediately agrees without protest
jokes.explanation: The joke is double-layered: Ilana's audacity in charging for public infrastructure, and Linda's complete lack of resistance. The parallel to the YouWork satire (paying for something that already exists) is structurally satisfying. Linda's immediate 'You know what? I'll take it.' is a great secondary beat.

jokes.text: Abbi tries to explain her plumbing issue; employee immediately pre-empts: 'Say no more. Bath accessories.'
jokes.setup: Abbi starts to describe her embarrassing toilet situation
jokes.punchline: Employee has heard enough — either he understands BB&B's limits or he genuinely doesn't need more info — and steers her away with smooth efficiency, no judgment
jokes.explanation: Economy is the best part — 'Say no more. Bath accessories.' is efficient and warm. The joke is in how well the employee reads her without needing details. Good small moment.

jokes.text: Ilana calls Abbi mid-BB&B trip: 'Can you grab me six power strips and six extension cords and meet me at that place where that dude gave you that condom stat?'
jokes.setup: Abbi is on a personal errand; Ilana is pivoting into a new scheme
jokes.punchline: Ilana uses a completely idiosyncratic location reference — 'that place where that dude gave you that condom stat' — that makes perfect sense only to these two, suggesting a rich shared history of weird NYC incidents
jokes.explanation: Excellent friendship shorthand joke. 'That place where that dude gave you that condom stat' is purely relational — it only works because these two have a shared history so full of bizarre incidents that THIS is a navigational landmark. Deeply character-specific to both women. High quotability for the writing room's imagination. Close to 8-range.

jokes.text: Abbi sees the setup Ilana has built and reacts: 'Holy shit bucket.'
jokes.setup: Earlier conversation about Abbi's shit bucket problem; now Abbi sees Ilana's chaotic SheWork setup
jokes.punchline: Abbi uses 'shit bucket' as a swear phrase (riff on 'holy shit') — an accidental/intentional callback that layers the episode's running motif onto a reaction moment
jokes.explanation: Tight, elegant callback. 'Holy shit bucket' functions as a perfectly formed composite expletive AND as a callback to the episode's running toilet problem. It's three syllables that do double comedic work. High quotability — the kind of thing viewers would adopt. Economy is near-perfect. Justifies 8.2: it's the kind of wordplay that only works because the episode has earned it structurally.

jokes.text: Ilana announces: 'Welcome to SheWork. We are officially open for shiz-ness.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is presenting her new venture
jokes.punchline: 'Open for shiz-ness' — a toned-down profanity pun on 'open for business', also functioning as a soft brand tagline
jokes.explanation: Minor pun, more cute than clever. 'Shiz-ness' is the kind of softened profanity that reads as brand-safe in a way that doesn't quite land as hard as Ilana's usual unfiltered language. Functional scene-opener.

jokes.text: Abbi's first impression of SheWork: 'I see a bunch of... no offense... trash furniture and two people smoking cigarettes.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just pitched SheWork as an 'inclusive work environment'
jokes.punchline: Abbi's clinical, parenthetical 'no offense' before 'trash furniture' is the joke — the polite gesture makes the bluntness funnier
jokes.explanation: The 'no offense' qualifier softening 'trash furniture' is the joke's engine. Abbi as the grounded observer of Ilana's schemes is a show staple, and this is a clean version of it. 'No offense... trash furniture' is good comic writing.

jokes.text: Ilana's SheWork pitch: 'If parkour is using the city as your gym, SheWork is using the city as your office.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is selling Abbi on SheWork's conceptual legitimacy
jokes.punchline: The parkour analogy is deadpan perfect — she's compared her sidewalk hustle to an extreme sport with total sincerity, using the structure of a Silicon Valley pitch
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode. The analogy is structurally perfect — it genuinely sounds like something from a WeWork pitch deck, which makes the absurdity land harder. Ilana's total sincerity is essential. The parallel construction ('X is using Y as your Z') is tight and memorable. High quotability. Justifies 8.2: the joke works on multiple levels simultaneously — it satirizes startup culture, reveals Ilana's self-mythology, and is genuinely a well-constructed analogy (the logic isn't wrong, just absurd in application).

jokes.text: Abbi: 'So, correct me if I'm wrong. People are paying you to use the free public charging stations?'
jokes.setup: Ilana has delivered her parkour pitch
jokes.punchline: Abbi strips away all the branding in one sentence — the joke is in the contrast between Ilana's elaborate framing and Abbi's reductive accuracy
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man line. The 'correct me if I'm wrong' setup makes it read as polite skepticism, which is funnier than open mockery. Works as a comeback to #32 and lands the satirical point cleanly.

jokes.text: Ilana's defense of SheWork: 'So what? All the great male entrepreneurs do it, okay? They take something that already exists, claim it was their idea, throw in some fancy furniture, and charge double for it.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just called out that Ilana is literally charging for public infrastructure
jokes.punchline: Ilana reframes her scam as feminist — by pointing out men do this constantly and get celebrated for it — which is both a good joke AND a legitimate observation about Silicon Valley
jokes.explanation: Sharp cultural commentary delivered as a character justification. The joke works on three levels: it's funny, it characterizes Ilana, and it makes a real point. The specificity of 'throw in some fancy furniture' is a nice concrete detail. Close to 8-range: the joke earns its political edge because it's embedded in character logic, not just written in as satire.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'For letting people sit on your junk and smoke?' Ilana: 'Exactly.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just made a feminist argument for her sidewalk hustle
jokes.punchline: Abbi re-distills it to its basest description; Ilana's proud 'Exactly' confirms she sees no contradiction
jokes.explanation: 'Sit on your junk and smoke' is funnier than any longer phrasing would be. Abbi's ongoing role as plain-spoken translator of Ilana's ideology is well-executed here. The one-word confirmation 'Exactly' is the right button.

jokes.text: Ilana on smoking: 'Okay, smoking is obvs heinous... But who am I to judge a nicotine addiction? I mean, I'm... fully addicted to weed.' Abbi: 'True.'
jokes.setup: Abbi challenges the ethics of SheWork enabling smoking
jokes.punchline: Ilana's moral relativism — she can't judge smokers because she's a committed weed addict — and Abbi's immediate, unconditional 'True' as validation
jokes.explanation: Abbi's single-word 'True' is the best part — it's the perfect best-friend confirmation that doesn't require explanation. The logic is genuinely Ilana's: her ethical framework runs through weed. High character integration score because this only works for Ilana.

jokes.text: The trash can Abbi brought from BB&B turns out to be her shit bucket: 'Actually, a trash can is the only thing we don't need here at SheWork.' 'No, no, no. This is actually mine, uh, my shit bucket.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is unloading BB&B supplies; a trash can is visible among them
jokes.punchline: The trash can isn't for SheWork — it's Abbi's shit bucket. Ilana's escalating 'Come again? ... Pardon me? ... My... I had to get a shit bucket.' — each clarification making it worse
jokes.explanation: Strong structural comedy. Ilana's triple request for clarification ('Come again? / Pardon me?') escalates the absurdity while confirming she genuinely cannot process what she's hearing. The callback lands because we've tracked the shit bucket from concept to physical reality. Abbi walking around NYC with a shit bucket is a great visual even read from transcript.

jokes.text: Ilana's response to the shit bucket reveal: 'Okay, um... Who, what, how, when, where, and why?'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just processed that Abbi bought and is carrying a shit bucket
jokes.punchline: She deploys the full journalistic Five Ws — plus 'how' — as her response, which is the correct instinct but delivered as comedy because the story doesn't actually require that much investigation
jokes.explanation: A good reaction beat. The deployment of all five Ws reads as Ilana's brain going to full investigative mode for a problem that is largely self-explanatory. The addition of 'how' (making it six questions) is a nice small escalation that shows the writers knew the six-question version was funnier than five.

jokes.text: Abbi confesses: 'I was standing there, and I was just, like, so stunned by what was going on, I got bullied into buying a shit bucket.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is explaining her purchase to Ilana
jokes.punchline: 'I got bullied into buying a shit bucket' — this construction (bully + shit bucket) is absurdist enough on its own, and Abbi's stunned resignation makes it land
jokes.explanation: Strong Abbi line. 'Bullied into buying a shit bucket' is a specific, vivid description of Abbi's relationship with authority and confrontation — she folds under mild social pressure into absurd outcomes. High character integration because this is quintessentially Abbi. Good quotability.

jokes.text: Ilana's scheme continued: plant the tit copies in Fernando's office, then say 'I-I think it might be Fernando, and while we're on the topic, why are we shitting in buckets?'
jokes.setup: Ilana is elaborating the tit-scan plan
jokes.punchline: The pivot from 'Fernando planted the boob copies' to 'and while we're on the topic, why are we shitting in buckets?' — the transition is the joke, linking two unrelated issues as if they follow naturally
jokes.explanation: The 'and while we're on the topic' transition is the funniest part — it's not on the topic at all. Ilana's brain is making connections that don't exist, and her commitment to the scheme makes it funnier. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Abbi counters Ilana's tit-scan plan with a boring grassroots petition: 'Okay, what if I print out a petition... I go around to my neighbors... I get the full signed petition, take it back to Fernando, and I'm like, Majority rules, man. Change is coming.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has proposed an elaborate/insane plan; Abbi offers an alternative
jokes.punchline: Abbi's alternative is completely reasonable — a signed petition — which lands as funny precisely because of the contrast with Ilana's scheme; 'Majority rules, man. Change is coming.' delivered with maximum earnest sincerity
jokes.explanation: The joke is mostly structural — Abbi's reasonable boringness as a counterpoint to Ilana's chaos. 'Majority rules, man. Change is coming.' is delivered with the gravity of a movement speech about a shit bucket problem, which is the comedy.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Ooh, you are full-blown grassroots. You are the Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez of Astoria. You're the Jewish Shirley Chisholm of toilets.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has proposed a basic petition
jokes.punchline: Ilana elevates Abbi to political sainthood through two increasingly specific and incongruous comparisons: AOC, then Jewish Shirley Chisholm, both 'of toilets'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The escalation from AOC to Shirley Chisholm (adding both the Jewish identity qualifier AND 'of toilets') is perfectly constructed absurdist hyperbole. 'The Jewish Shirley Chisholm of toilets' is an extraordinarily specific compliment that only makes sense in the Ilana-Abbi friendship context. High quotability — this is the kind of phrase you repeat. Justifies 8.0: the joke has a logical escalation structure, is character-specific, and the final 'of toilets' lands the degradation that makes the hyperbole funny.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Yes, I am gonna shit in my toilet and flush.' Ilana: 'Yes. Go get your bucket and take care of your shit.'
jokes.setup: Abbi's declaration of independence after the grassroots pep talk
jokes.punchline: 'Take care of your shit' functions simultaneously as (1) a literal instruction about the bucket and (2) idiomatic encouragement to handle her business — the double meaning is perfect
jokes.explanation: The dual meaning works cleanly. 'Take care of your shit' is a motivational send-off and a literal toilet instruction simultaneously. Good economy; the scene button is well-constructed. The repetition of 'Take care of your shit. / Take care of your shit.' gives it the rhythm of a sendoff catchphrase.

jokes.text: Ilana checks on Linda: 'How's that Wi-Fi working, L.L.?' Linda: 'Ha, ha. Charging Station-Guest is coming in clear as day.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is performing the duties of a co-working space manager
jokes.punchline: Linda names the free public Wi-Fi network — 'Charging Station-Guest' — confirming she knows exactly what she's paying for and is fine with it
jokes.explanation: Good callback execution. The specificity of 'Charging Station-Guest' as the Wi-Fi network name is perfect — it sounds exactly like a city-provided network, confirming the con without needing to explain it. Linda's cheerful complicity completes the joke.

jokes.text: Ilana asks Linda to 'watch the fort' while she expands. Linda's 'Would I?' — Ilana interprets it as enthusiasm; Linda clearly meant it as a rhetorical 'no.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is promoting Linda to de facto manager of a sidewalk
jokes.punchline: 'Would I?' read as eager affirmation by Ilana, but Linda's pause — and Ilana's 'No, I-I was asking...' — reveals it was a rhetorical 'no.' The beat between them is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A clean ambiguity joke that works on the awkward beat. Ilana's social obliviousness makes her read 'Would I?' as excited affirmation; the pause and clarification are funny because the miscommunication is never fully resolved. Strong structure — the setup and subversion are in adjacent lines.

jokes.text: Neighbor opens the door: 'I don't want to buy any Girl Scout Cookies.' Abbi: 'No, no, no. I-I'm not a Girl Scout anymore.'
jokes.setup: Abbi knocks on a neighbor's door to discuss building issues
jokes.punchline: The neighbor mistakes her for a Girl Scout; Abbi's correction — 'I'm not a Girl Scout anymore' — implies she WAS a Girl Scout until recently, or at least that this happens to her regularly
jokes.explanation: Good joke. The 'anymore' implies a history that the audience doesn't need explained but finds funny. Abbi's guilelessness — she just accepts that she looks like a Girl Scout — is character-consistent. The neighbor's bluntness is a good NYC detail.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'No, no, no. I-I'm not a Girl Scout anymore. I'm a grown... I-I'm actually your neighbor.'
jokes.setup: Neighbor thinks Abbi is a Girl Scout
jokes.punchline: Abbi's self-correction 'I'm not a Girl Scout ANYMORE' implies she was one recently enough that it needs to be clarified, and 'I'm a grown...' trails off as if she's convincing herself
jokes.explanation: The 'anymore' is the word that makes this. It implies she was one recently enough that she needs to specify it's past tense. Then 'I'm a grown...' trailing off — she can't even finish the sentence. Self-undermining confidence is a great Abbi bit.

jokes.text: Abbi tries to say she's their neighbor; neighbor says 'I have lived in this building for 31 years, and I've never seen you.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to establish credibility as a neighbor
jokes.punchline: 31 years of residence deployed as a credential-crushing statement — the neighbor has no doubt and zero interest in being disputed
jokes.explanation: Functional NYC-elder joke. The 31-year credential is a specific, good detail. Not exceptional but consistent with the neighbor character's dismissive energy.

jokes.text: Abbi tries to pivot to the plumbing problem but the neighbor attacks oat milk and Sriracha: 'You hippies are the reason that sandwiches in this neighborhood cost $16 with your Sriracha and your oat milk. What's wrong with cow milk?!'
jokes.setup: Abbi tries to get back on topic about plumbing
jokes.punchline: The neighbor goes on an unprompted $16 sandwich rant about gentrification, landing on cow milk as her hill to die on
jokes.explanation: A good character moment for the neighbor. '$16 sandwiches' and the specificity of 'your Sriracha and your oat milk' are good details. The 'What's wrong with cow milk?!' as the peak of outrage is a funny place to land. Not exceptional but entertaining.

jokes.text: Neighbor praises Bevers for bringing 'delicious cheeses'; Abbi has to confirm 'That's... That's Bevers'
jokes.setup: Neighbor describes Melody's 'wonderful young man who always brings me delicious cheeses'
jokes.punchline: It's Bevers — a character apparently well-regarded in the building despite being Abbi's least favorite presence in her own home. Abbi's flat 'That's Bevers' carries suppressed anguish
jokes.explanation: Good character joke requiring some prior knowledge of Bevers (Abbi's nemesis-adjacent roommate situation). The neighbor's warmth for Bevers — apparently a cheese bringer! — is the right amount of absurd detail. Abbi's pained acknowledgment 'They're the loveliest. I love them, as well' is Abbi performing civic grace while internally screaming.

jokes.text: Neighbor: 'Cow's milk makes me have to shit!' Abbi: 'Which is what I came here to talk to you about in the first place.'
jokes.setup: Neighbor has been on an unrelated tangent about milk and gentrification
jokes.punchline: The neighbor's digression lands on 'shit' — and Abbi pivots the scatological complaint directly back to the toilet problem — a perfect circular return
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's tightest jokes. The structure is near-perfect: an unrelated rant about cow milk accidentally returns to the episode's central problem ('makes me have to shit'), and Abbi's callback to her original purpose ('Which is what I came here to talk to you about') is an elegant comedic judo move. The joke works on structure alone — the content happens to align. Justifies 8.3: it's the best structural joke in the episode outside of the negotiation scene, connecting disparate threads with a single line.

jokes.text: Cut to Ilana at SheWork, clearly flourishing: 'Ooh, God, I'm rich. This city's full of trash.'
jokes.setup: SheWork appears to be attracting clients
jokes.punchline: 'This city's full of trash' — double meaning: (1) the city has trash on it, from which Ilana is literally profiting, and (2) city residents are 'trash' people who pay $0.50/minute for public sidewalk access
jokes.explanation: Sharp and double-layered. The economy is excellent — two sentences doing a lot of work. 'I'm rich' lands as pure Ilana self-mythology, and 'full of trash' is both literally and figuratively accurate in context. High quotability.

jokes.text: Abbi enters the tenant meeting and announces: 'I know you're looking at me like I'm the hot, young new kid on the block.' Neighbor: 'You're young?'
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to establish herself as a relatable young person to this older building
jokes.punchline: Someone immediately questions whether 'young' applies — likely the 31-year resident — which deflates Abbi's self-perception in one word
jokes.explanation: Clean and well-timed. The 'You're young?' subversion of Abbi's self-presentation is quick and efficient. A crowd that old vs. a 30-year-old calling herself young is a relatable comic observation.

jokes.text: One neighbor recognizes Abbi: 'I know you. 4H, right?' Then: 'Oh, you're the chick who pegged Jeremy.' Multiple neighbors chime in with details about Jeremy's moaning.
jokes.setup: Abbi needs a neighbor to vouch for her identity
jokes.punchline: Her identity is confirmed not by being a neighbor but by having pegged Jeremy — and several people apparently heard it, including detailed testimony about it sounding like 'an Earth-shattering orgasm'
jokes.explanation: Strong escalation joke. The reveal that 'the chick who pegged Jeremy' is Abbi's building identity is both surprising and character-consistent (Abbi as sexually adventurous but publicly restrained). The neighbor's testimony — 'I heard the moaning. You got him good. Sounded like an Earth-shattering orgasm.' — is funny in its clinical detail. Good building-community texture.

jokes.text: Abbi's community rally speech: 'Maybe you only know me as the chick who pegged Jeremy. I'm... I'm fine with that.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has had her sexual history weaponized at her own meeting
jokes.punchline: She accepts it, pivots, and tries to use it as a leadership credential — 'If you can trust Jeremy to let her peg, we can probably trust her'
jokes.explanation: The joke lands on two levels: Abbi's graceful acceptance of her sexual reputation as a civic credential, and the neighbor's logic — 'if he let her peg, we can probably trust her' — which is an absurd but internally consistent endorsement. 'I'm... I'm fine with that' is Abbi performing acceptance she's still processing, which is great performance writing. The fact that pegging-consent becomes civic trustworthiness evidence is the kind of specific, sex-positive absurdism Broad City does at its best.

jokes.text: After Abbi's impassioned 'We can be the change we wish to see — let's say no to shit buckets,' one neighbor says: 'I love Jeremy. If he let her peg, we can probably trust her.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has delivered an earnest activist speech about plumbing rights
jokes.punchline: The neighbor's endorsement is entirely based on Jeremy's sexual choices — the logic being: if Jeremy trusted her that much, the building can too
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. 'If he let her peg, we can probably trust her' is a perfectly absurdist endorsement that follows impeccable internal logic (sexual trust = civic trust). It's subversive, quotable, character-specific to the NYC elder neighbor, and lands as a callback payoff to the pegging revelation. The joke works on rewatch because you see it coming from the Jeremy reveal and still it lands hard. Justifies 8.3/9.0: the economy is near-perfect, the logic is both absurd and internally consistent, and it's highly quotable.

jokes.text: The neighbor's endorsement leads to the full building's buy-in — 'All right. / My man.' — and Abbi immediately says 'Let's take a quick pic, cause what is activism without posting it on Instagram, right? I don't know if it exists.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just successfully mobilized her neighbors
jokes.punchline: She immediately reduces the moment to an Instagram photo op — 'I don't know if it exists' as the punchline, implying unposted activism may not be real
jokes.explanation: 'I don't know if it exists' is a good line — the Schrödinger's activism joke about social media has been made before but the delivery and timing here is clean. Character-consistent for Abbi's millennial self-awareness.

jokes.text: Ilana has acquired a new client; when Linda sees: 'Linda, you exquisite genius! May I kiss you?' Linda says yes; Ilana does, then: 'Mm, smoky. So tender.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is celebrating a new client signing
jokes.punchline: Ilana's joy escalates to an actual kiss on Linda, then sensory commentary on Linda's smoker's mouth — 'Mm, smoky. So tender.' — delivered as a genuine compliment
jokes.explanation: High character integration — Ilana's boundary-free physical affection and her capacity to find beauty in Linda's smoky mouth is quintessential Ilana. 'Smoky. So tender.' is funny because it's exactly how you'd describe a steak, and Ilana means it as the sincerest compliment. Good cringe-warmth balance.

jokes.text: Abbi calls Fernando in for a meeting at SheWork, treating the outdoor sidewalk setup as a formal business conference.
jokes.setup: Abbi has organized tenant resistance; now she summons Fernando
jokes.punchline: The visual of a formal tenant meeting being held at an outdoor sidewalk co-working space made of trash furniture — Abbi performing corporate authority in the most absurd venue possible
jokes.explanation: Visual comedy inferred from context — the formal meeting held on a sidewalk with trash furniture is a good sight gag. Works better on screen than in transcript. Functions as setup for the petition reveal.

jokes.text: Abbi presents Fernando with the petition and says 'Change is gonna come, my friend.' Then: 'I think you're gonna want to take a look at this.' Fernando looks, then: 'This is not good.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has triumphantly presented her petition to Fernando
jokes.punchline: Fernando's 'This is not good' suggests either the petition is going sideways or what he's seeing isn't what she intended — the deflation of her victory is staged but builds to the bigger joke
jokes.explanation: Transitional beat building to the Flushing/developing nation joke. Functions as setup more than standalone. Fernando's deflation of Abbi's triumph is expected but executed competently.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Fernando, no disrespect to your country...' Fernando: 'I'm from Flushing. I grew up 10 minutes from here.' Abbi: 'I apologize.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is making an argument to Fernando about living conditions, presumably invoking developing-world comparisons again
jokes.punchline: She makes an assumption about Fernando's national origin; he calmly corrects her — he's from Flushing, Queens — and Abbi immediately and flatly apologizes with no self-justification
jokes.explanation: Strong callback that deepens Abbi's character flaw (she keeps making this exact assumption about Fernando) while being structurally tight. 'I'm from Flushing' is a perfect geographical grounding — both a real place and, in context, a toilet pun. Fernando's dignity is consistent. Abbi's clean 'I apologize' without excuse is the right button. Rewatch bonus because you see her making the same mistake again.

jokes.text: After Fernando agrees to pass along the demands, Abbi says to herself: 'I'm the Rosa Parks of poop.' Fernando: 'What?'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just won her negotiation with Fernando
jokes.punchline: 'I'm the Rosa Parks of poop' — Abbi's triumphant self-comparison, delivered as an internal thought Fernando unfortunately catches; the 'What?' is the audience surrogate
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy. Six words, maximum absurdity. Abbi has internalized Ilana's civic-rights framing of her toilet protest to its logical extreme. This is the payoff to the 'Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez of Astoria / Jewish Shirley Chisholm of toilets' joke — Abbi has promoted herself to Rosa Parks. Fernando's confused 'What?' is the right button. Very quotable: 'I'm the Rosa Parks of poop' is precisely the kind of phrase that sticks.

jokes.text: Ilana runs SheWork like a full business, greeting clients by name: 'Morty, you want a fresh one? And, Ms. Katherine, anything for you? Yes, girl. Bev, anything? She on a diet. I get it. James, anything? Got a birthday coming up. Debbie, snack, smoke?'
jokes.setup: SheWork has grown; Ilana is now managing multiple clients on the sidewalk
jokes.punchline: Ilana knows everyone's names, dietary status, and birthdays — she has fully committed to this as a real business and become a sidewalk concierge who 'reads' her clients
jokes.explanation: Good sustained character beat. 'She on a diet. I get it.' and 'Got a birthday coming up' delivered with the authority of a maître d' shows Ilana at peak confidence in her absurd enterprise. Character integration is high — Ilana's charismatic relational intelligence is on full display.

jokes.text: 'I'm not a business bitch. I am a business, bitch! SheWork, where your lungs are your goddamn business.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is on a roll managing SheWork
jokes.punchline: Riff on Jay-Z's 'I'm not a businessman, I'm a business, man' applied to the SheWork context — 'where your lungs are your goddamn business' as a brand slogan for a smoker's co-working space
jokes.explanation: The Jay-Z riff is a well-known enough reference that it lands cleanly. The addition of 'SheWork, where your lungs are your goddamn business' as a tagline is creative and specific. High quotability. Character integration is strong — Ilana's fluency in hip-hop cultural capital and her business self-mythology come together perfectly.

jokes.text: Ilana threatens YouWork: 'I hooked up with someone very high up in the human resources department of Twitter once.' YouWork rep: 'One second to side bar?'
jokes.setup: YouWork is threatening legal action against SheWork
jokes.punchline: Ilana's counter-threat is that she had sex with an HR person at Twitter once — the logic being that she could start a PR fire — and the YouWork reps immediately take it seriously enough to confer
jokes.explanation: Strong joke. The specific detail — 'human resources department' (not even the CEO) — makes Ilana's threat both absurd and somehow credible. The fact that YouWork immediately asks to confer validates that social media threats are real corporate currency. Character integration is high: Ilana's sexual history as leverage is a very Broad City move.

jokes.text: YouWork reps confer: 'I don't want to fuck with Twitter again.' 'I don't like the sound of that.' 'Plus we have to play them in soccer this weekend.' 'Oh, my God. We'd never hear the end of it.'
jokes.setup: The reps are evaluating Ilana's threat
jokes.punchline: The corporate calculus escalates from PR fear to the very real concern of losing a soccer game to Twitter and hearing about it — the mundane athletic rivalry is funnier than any legal concern
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best surprise laughs. The tech-bro corporate culture joke escalates from the expected (PR anxiety) to the absurd specific (inter-company soccer game). 'We'd never hear the end of it' is the perfect button — these two grown businessmen are more afraid of Twitter's sports gloating than any legal action. Justifies 8.1: the escalation is unexpected, the specificity of 'soccer this weekend' is excellent, and it characterizes the tech-bro world efficiently.

jokes.text: YouWork offers Ilana $100 to go away. The negotiation: '$100 / $900 / $100 / $800' down to '$500, and that's my final offer. Fine.'
jokes.setup: YouWork needs Ilana to disappear
jokes.punchline: Ilana negotiates DOWN from $900 to $500 through a bidding war she's running the wrong direction — a perfect callback to her negotiating UP at the co-working space opening
jokes.explanation: The best structured joke in the episode. The callback to the opening negotiation scene is perfect: Ilana negotiated herself UP to $1,000 when she should have paid $500; now she negotiates DOWN from $900 to $500 when she should have held firm. The symmetry is elegant — she is equally bad at negotiating in opposite directions. Structure is near-perfect because the joke requires the earlier scene to work, and when it does, it rewards attention. Both instances show Ilana with complete confidence operating the wrong framework. Justifies 8.7: the structural payoff, character specificity, and economy of the rapid-fire exchange are all exceptional.

jokes.text: After accepting $500, Abbi says: 'I guess we're not getting a mani-mani-pedi.' Ilana: 'Man. Yeah, yeah, mm, mm. It's Friday. That's our thing. I know it is, but it's okay.'
jokes.setup: They've just taken a $500 settlement instead of fighting
jokes.punchline: The thing they're mourning with genuine sadness is a weekly manicure-pedi ritual — the contrast between their earlier activist/entrepreneurial ambitions and the simple pleasure they gave up is the joke
jokes.explanation: 'That's our thing' delivered with quiet sincerity is a perfect small moment. The episode has been about big schemes and activism, and now we remember these are just two friends with a Friday ritual. Good emotional texture that earns a small laugh through recognition.

jokes.text: Ilana thanks her SheWork clients: 'You made me realize how beautiful yellow teeth and brown fingernails and hair that smells like soot can be, so thank you for that.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is giving an emotional closing speech to her sidewalk clients
jokes.punchline: What she 'learned' from SheWork is an appreciation for the aesthetic of heavy smokers — yellow teeth, brown nails, soot hair — delivered as a sincere compliment and life lesson
jokes.explanation: Very Ilana. She has found the beauty in something objectively unpleasant and is sincerely grateful for it. The specificity of 'hair that smells like soot' as a positive is the best detail. Character integration is extremely high — only Ilana would close a business by complimenting yellow teeth.

jokes.text: After the big shutdown, a client reveals: 'I paid a week in advance.' Ilana: 'You know, the market is just so up and down, brother. It's, you know, the market.'
jokes.setup: SheWork has been shut down on its first day
jokes.punchline: Ilana explains away the client's prepayment with the abstract 'the market' — it's what every startup uses to explain failure, applied to a sidewalk that lasted one afternoon
jokes.explanation: Excellent character button for the SheWork arc. Ilana's deployment of 'the market' as cover for a same-day collapse is perfect startup culture parody, and her double use of 'the market' with the vague sincerity of a VC is funny. The call-back to entrepreneur-speak is satisfying.

jokes.text: The abandoned Linda subplot: SheWork is closing; Linda's computer has been stolen and she can't find her son. 'Where's my computer? / Who took my computer? / My screenplay! / Christian? / Where's my kid?'
jokes.setup: SheWork is shutting down; Ilana has already mentally moved on
jokes.punchline: Linda's escalating losses — computer, screenplay, then CHILD — culminate in the unhinged revelation that she left her son unsupervised on a public sidewalk long enough for him to disappear
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with a genuinely dark button. The child being an afterthought after the screenplay is the correct order of priorities for Linda's character. 'Christian? / Where's my kid?' as two separate, escalating panic lines is well-structured. Dark but earned by Linda's characterization.

jokes.text: Abbi has been using the shit bucket AS an actual toilet: 'Man, the beauty of that bucket is that I'm squatting so low to reach it, so I'm, like, in Squatty Potty position. It's almost like a two-for-one.' Ilana: 'Hold on. You shit in the bucket? You're only supposed to put toilet paper in the bucket.'
jokes.setup: The shit bucket has been an episode-long thread; Abbi made the purchase based on Fernando's description, which she apparently misunderstood
jokes.punchline: Abbi has been defecating in a trash can in her apartment and framing it as a wellness practice ('Squatty Potty position'); the reveal that she fundamentally misunderstood the bucket's purpose recontextualizes every earlier shit bucket moment
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode and one of the best structural payoffs in the series. The entire shit bucket thread — Fernando's description, Abbi's impulse purchase, her carrying it to BB&B and SheWork, Ilana's confusion about it — all exists to set up this single revelation: Abbi misunderstood and has been literally defecating in a bucket, which she then immediately reframed as superior posture technology ('Squatty Potty position'). The 'two-for-one' construction is perfect. The rewatch bonus is enormous — every earlier shit bucket reference lands harder knowing this. Justifies 8.8: the episode has been carefully structured around this payoff; it surprises on first watch, rewards on rewatch, is character-specific (Abbi's passive acceptance of Fernando's authority leading to this), and the 'Squatty Potty' rationalization is an excellent additional comedic layer.

jokes.text: After learning she's been using the bucket wrong: 'See... Okay. Shit. Shit, shit, shit, shit, shit.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just been corrected about the shit bucket
jokes.punchline: 'Shit' repeated five times in escalating panic — both a reaction to her mistake and an accidental/intentional word choice that doubles as the literal subject of the error
jokes.explanation: A good button on the shit bucket reveal. The panicked repetition of 'shit' — working simultaneously as profanity and literal descriptor — is economical and funny. The escalation from one 'shit' to five captures Abbi processing the full implication of her weeks (?) of bucket-pooping.

jokes.text: Abbi's response to realizing she's been misusing the bucket: 'Okay. Shit. Shit, shit, shit, shit, shit.'
jokes.setup: Abbi realizes she's been shitting in the bucket
jokes.punchline: 'Shit, shit, shit, shit, shit' — the expletive cascade is literally what she's been doing in the bucket and also her emotional response to the revelation
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's most economical joke. 'Shit, shit, shit, shit, shit' works on three levels simultaneously: (1) expletive cascade expressing distress, (2) literal description of what she has been doing, (3) a callback to the entire episode's central comedic thread. Maximum meaning per word (it's only ONE word, repeated), strongest possible character integration (only Abbi would accidentally do this), and the episode-long setup means the payoff has enormous structural weight. This and joke 65 form the episode's comedic apex.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Those assholes! They stole my idea. I knew I should've done an equity split.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just seen YouWork steal her concept
jokes.punchline: Her response is not 'I should have gotten a patent' or 'I should have fought harder' — it's 'I knew I should've done an equity split' — the logic of someone whose framework for protecting ideas is Silicon Valley deal-making, not legal recourse
jokes.explanation: Good character specificity. 'Equity split' is the correct Silicon Valley term deployed by someone who clearly doesn't have enough leverage to demand one. Ilana's vocabulary outpacing her actual power is a consistent character note.

jokes.text: Abbi's pep talk: 'We are still two badass activated women. I mean, Ilana, you founded and sold a company in one day.' Ilana: 'That's right.'
jokes.setup: They've lost SheWork and Ilana's idea has been stolen
jokes.punchline: Abbi reframes the failure as an achievement: Ilana 'founded and sold a company in one day' — technically true, but the company was a sidewalk, and the 'sale' was a $500 settlement to go away
jokes.explanation: Clean ironic reframing. 'Founded and sold a company in one day' is technically accurate and completely misleading. The warmth of Abbi's supportive friendship makes it funnier — she means it as genuine encouragement and Ilana accepts it at face value. Good character work.
